title: Best VPN for Business and Remote Work 2026: Team Security That Actually Scales
description: Consumer VPNs are not built for teams. This guide covers the best business VPN options for 2026, from NordLayer to Cisco AnyConnect, with a focus on central management, compliance, and remote access.
content: When a single remote worker uses a VPN, the requirements are simple: speed, privacy, and reliability. When a company of 50 sends all employees home, the requirements change completely. You need centralized user management, audit logs for compliance, dedicated IP addresses for office network access, and the ability to provision and revoke access without asking employees to reinstall anything. Consumer VPN products are not designed for any of this. Business VPN is a different product category, and choosing the wrong type wastes money while leaving gaps in your security posture.

The core distinction between consumer and business VPN comes down to three things. First, accountability: consumer VPNs have no concept of who in your organization is connected, from what device, and what they accessed. Business VPNs produce logs that can be audited, which matters for ISO 27001, SOC 2, and GDPR compliance. Second, access control: a consumer VPN tunnels all traffic through a shared server. A business VPN can enforce that the sales team accesses the CRM but not the internal financial systems. Third, provisioning: consumer VPNs require each user to create their own account and manage their own credentials. Business VPNs integrate with your existing identity provider, Active Directory, or SSO solution, so onboarding and offboarding are handled centrally.

NordLayer is the business-grade product from the team behind NordVPN, and it is the strongest option for companies that want familiar consumer-VPN performance with proper team management built on top. The central dashboard lets you create user groups, assign network access policies per group, and see connection activity across your whole team. NordLayer supports dedicated servers per organization, which means your company gets a fixed IP address that you can whitelist on internal systems, cloud platforms, and third-party services. Pricing starts around $7 per user per month and scales down for larger teams. The mobile apps and desktop clients are clean and well-maintained, and they work reliably in most countries including restrictive markets. For companies under 200 employees that want to stop managing VPN accounts manually, NordLayer is the practical first choice.

Perimeter 81, now part of Check Point, takes a zero-trust approach that goes beyond what a traditional VPN does. Instead of tunneling all traffic through a single gateway, it grants access on a per-application or per-resource basis. An employee working from home can access the internal project management system and nothing else, with no full network tunnel opened. This is called ZTNA, zero-trust network access, and it is increasingly what enterprise security teams require when auditors ask how remote access is controlled. Perimeter 81 integrates natively with Azure AD, Okta, Google Workspace, and other identity providers, so access rights follow the user's existing account permissions. It is more complex to configure than NordLayer, and pricing is higher, but for companies where compliance is a hard requirement rather than a nice-to-have, it is the right architecture.

Cisco AnyConnect is the enterprise standard. If your company already runs Cisco networking infrastructure, AnyConnect is likely already in the budget and on the IT roadmap. It is the most widely supported VPN client in corporate environments, which matters when you need to integrate with firewall policies, RADIUS authentication servers, or legacy internal systems that expect a specific client. The tradeoff is cost and complexity: AnyConnect is not a self-serve product. It requires network engineering to deploy properly and ongoing administration to maintain. For large enterprises with dedicated IT teams, that is fine. For a company of 30 people without a full-time network engineer, it is overkill and the management overhead will offset the security benefit.

ExpressVPN Teams targets smaller businesses that want a faster path to coverage without complex infrastructure. Setup takes minutes per user, the management dashboard covers the basics, and the underlying consumer VPN technology is among the fastest available. It does not offer the zero-trust access control of Perimeter 81 or the dedicated server infrastructure of NordLayer, but for a 5 to 15 person company where the main requirement is encrypting traffic on public networks and accessing geo-restricted services, it is a reasonable choice at a lower price point than enterprise alternatives.

Split tunneling is one of the most important features to get right in a business VPN deployment, and it is often misconfigured. When split tunneling is off, all employee traffic routes through the VPN, including personal browsing, streaming, and family devices on the same home network. This creates unnecessary load on company infrastructure and raises privacy questions about monitoring personal activity. When split tunneling is configured correctly, only traffic destined for company resources routes through the VPN. Personal internet traffic goes directly to the internet. This keeps bandwidth usage reasonable, reduces latency for non-work applications, and is clearer about what the company is and is not monitoring. Every business VPN product in this guide supports split tunneling, but it usually requires manual configuration to set up properly.

A note on why consumer VPNs do not work for teams, even if employees already pay for them personally. No audit logs means no compliance evidence. Shared IP addresses mean you cannot whitelist a single company IP at your bank, cloud provider, or payment processor. No centralized billing means employees expense personal accounts, which creates accounting complexity and leaves the company in the dark if someone cancels their subscription. No device policy enforcement means employees can connect from unmanaged devices with no security baseline. These are not theoretical concerns. They are gaps that come up in every security audit. A business VPN costs more than a consumer product, but the management overhead it removes is worth the price difference for any company that takes its security posture seriously.
